stem: The patient with exacerbation of COPD is experiencing increased shortness of breath and wheezing. Based on the ABG results, prioritize the following interventions in the order they should be implemented.

rationale.correct: The correct sequence follows the Airway, Breathing, Circulation (ABC) framework, prioritizing the most immediate life-threatening conditions. The patient's ABGs show severe hypoxemia (PaO2 55 mmHg) and acute respiratory acidosis (pH 7.30, PaCO2 60 mmHg), which must be addressed immediately. Oxygen administration is the first priority to correct hypoxemia. Next, a bronchodilator is given to open the airways and improve ventilation. Corticosteroids follow to reduce inflammation, a slower process. Airway clearance techniques are next, followed by monitoring (repeat ABGs) to evaluate the effectiveness of the interventions.

answerBreakdown.option: Administer oxygen via nasal cannula and titrate to SpO2 > 90%.
answerBreakdown.rationale: Step 1: This is the highest priority. The patient's PaO2 of 55 mmHg indicates severe hypoxemia, a direct threat to life. Correcting this is the most critical first step based on the ABCs (Breathing).

Step 2: After starting oxygen, the underlying cause of poor gas exchange—bronchoconstriction—must be addressed. A short-acting beta-agonist and anticholinergic combination provides rapid bronchodilation, improving airflow and reducing the work of breathing.

Step 3: Airway inflammation is a key component of a COPD exacerbation. While essential, the anti-inflammatory effects of corticosteroids are not as immediate as oxygen or bronchodilators. Administering them IV is the next step to begin reducing inflammation and preventing further worsening.

answerBreakdown.option: Encourage coughing and deep breathing exercises.
answerBreakdown.rationale: Step 4: Once immediate life threats are managed with medication, airway clearance techniques can be implemented. These exercises help mobilize secretions that obstruct smaller airways, improving ventilation and preventing atelectasis. This is done after the airways are more open from the nebulizer.

Step 5: This is an evaluation step. After implementing all initial interventions, it is crucial to assess their effectiveness. A repeat ABG will show if oxygenation and ventilation have improved and will guide further treatment adjustments. Monitoring comes after intervening.

mnemonic.name: O-B-C-A-M
mnemonic.description: Oxygen, Bronchodilator, Corticosteroid, Airway Clearance, Monitor. This mnemonic helps remember the priority of interventions for a COPD exacerbation.

rationale.incorrect: Any other sequence incorrectly prioritizes care. For example, performing airway clearance before administering a bronchodilator is less effective as the airways are still constricted. Monitoring ABGs before administering oxygen delays a life-saving intervention for a patient with a PaO2 of 55 mmHg, which could lead to respiratory arrest.

questionTrap.reason: This question tests the ability to distinguish between immediate, life-saving interventions and important but less urgent actions. All options are correct interventions for COPD, but the order is critical.
questionTrap.commonMistake: A common error is to choose monitoring (ABGs) as a first step. While assessment is key, in a crisis with clear evidence of severe hypoxemia, intervention (administering oxygen) must come first. Another mistake is prioritizing steroids over fast-acting bronchodilators.

clinicalPearls: In a COPD exacerbation, the primary goals are to correct hypoxemia and reverse bronchospasm.
While oxygen is vital for COPD patients, it should be titrated carefully to a target SpO2 of 90-92% to avoid suppressing the hypoxic respiratory drive, which can lead to hypercapnia.
Systemic corticosteroids require several hours to take effect but are crucial for reducing the underlying inflammation and preventing relapse of the exacerbation.

tabs.title: Clinical Feed
tabs.content:
Admission Note - 12:00
S: Patient is a 68-year-old male with a known history of COPD, admitted from home for acute-onset, severe dyspnea. He reports a productive cough with yellow sputum for 2 days, which worsened significantly this morning.
B: History of COPD, hypertension, and type 2 diabetes. His adult child, at the bedside, states he has been using his rescue inhalers more frequently without relief. Patient reports living in a neighborhood with high levels of industrial air pollution, which he feels exacerbates his condition.
A: Appears anxious, speaking in 3-word sentences, and using accessory muscles. Initial SpO2 on room air was 89%. Bilateral expiratory wheezes noted.
R: Admit to medical unit for management of COPD exacerbation. Start continuous pulse oximetry. Obtain STAT ABG and chest x-ray.

Progress Note - 14:00
S: Patient reports his breathing is getting worse.
B: Admitted 2 hours ago for COPD exacerbation.
A: Work of breathing has increased. He remains anxious and tachypneic at 30/min. Wheezing is more pronounced. SpO2 has dropped to 88% on room air. STAT ABG results received and show acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis with significant hypoxemia.
R: Provider notified of ABG results. New STAT orders received to manage acute respiratory distress. The priority is to improve oxygenation and ventilation immediately to prevent respiratory failure.

tabs.title: Lab Diagnostics
tabs.content:
Arterial Blood Gas (ABG) - Drawn at 14:00

Result      | Value | Unit    | Reference Range | Status
----------------------------------------------------------------
pH          | 7.30  |         | 7.35 - 7.45     | Low
PaCO2       | 60    | mmHg    | 35 - 45         | High
PaO2        | 55    | mmHg    | 80 - 100        | Low
HCO3        | 30    | mEq/L   | 22 - 26         | High
SaO2 (calc) | 85    | %       | 95 - 100        | Low

tabs.title: Physical Exam
tabs.content:
14:00 Focused Respiratory Assessment

General: Anxious male, sitting in tripod position. Speaking in 3-word phrases.
Respiratory: Tachypneic with a rate of 30/min. Significant use of accessory muscles (sternocleidomastoid, intercostals). Diffuse expiratory wheezing auscultated bilaterally. Coarse crackles at lung bases. Productive cough with thick, yellow sputum.
Cardiovascular: Tachycardia with a regular rhythm. S1/S2 audible. No murmurs or gallops.

tabs.title: Radiology
tabs.content:
Chest X-ray (PA and Lateral) - Report from 12:30

FINDINGS: The lungs are hyperinflated with flattened hemidiaphragms, consistent with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. There are no focal consolidations, effusions, or pneumothorax to suggest an acute pneumonia or other complicating process. Increased bronchial wall markings are noted throughout both lungs, indicative of chronic bronchitis. The cardiomediastinal silhouette is within normal limits.

IMPRESSION: Chronic changes of severe COPD. No acute cardiopulmonary process identified.

tabs.title: Care Plan
tabs.content:
Order Date/Time | Action                                                      | Frequency/Status
--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Today @ 14:05   | Oxygen via nasal cannula, titrate to maintain SpO2 90-92%   | STAT
Today @ 14:05   | Albuterol-Ipratropium 2.5mg/0.5mg nebulizer treatment       | STAT
Today @ 14:05   | Methylprednisolone 40 mg IV                                 | STAT
Today @ 14:05   | Obtain repeat ABG 1 hour after oxygen therapy initiated     | STAT
Today @ 12:10   | Lisinopril 20mg                                             | Daily
Today @ 12:10   | Metformin 500mg                                             | Twice Daily

tabs.title: MAR Console
tabs.content:
Medication                  | Dose    | Route     | Frequency   | Last Administered
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Albuterol Inhaler           | 2 puffs | Inhaled   | PRN         | Today @ 12:00
Ipratropium Inhaler         | 2 puffs | Inhaled   | PRN         | Today @ 12:00
Lisinopril                  | 20 mg   | PO        | Daily       | Today @ 08:00
Metformin                   | 500 mg  | PO        | Twice Daily | Today @ 08:00

correctResponse: Administer oxygen via nasal cannula and titrate to SpO2 > 90%.
Encourage coughing and deep breathing exercises.
possibleResponses: Administer oxygen via nasal cannula and titrate to SpO2 > 90%.
